summary: The Docker-based reproduction confirms that the parse_shortlink.py expand_url_via_redirect_header function makes HTTP requests to user-controlled domains without validation. A mock internal service at 127.0.0.1:18888 received an HTTP GET request from the Unfurl application, confirming the SSRF.
expected_security_impact: The reproduction should cause Unfurl to make an HTTP request to a simulated internal service at 127.0.0.1:18888, demonstrating that user-controlled domain names from URLs flow into outbound requests.get() calls without validation.
actual_observed_result: Running ./reproduction/run.sh built and launched the Docker container. The exploit_trigger.py started a mock HTTP server on 127.0.0.1:18888, then called expand_url_via_redirect_header('http://127.0.0.1:18888/', 'ssrf-test'). The mock server received the request and responded with a 302 redirect, confirming the SSRF. The success marker /tmp/ssrf_success.txt was written.
verification_method: The reproduction starts a mock HTTP server on 127.0.0.1:18888 inside the container, then calls the vulnerable expand_url_via_redirect_header function with a URL pointing to that server. verify.sh checks for the presence of /tmp/ssrf_success.txt, which is written by the mock server when it receives a request. This distinguishes a real SSRF from a startup failure or unrelated error.
evidence_produced_by_run: Mock HTTP server at 127.0.0.1:18888 received GET /ssrf-test from the Unfurl application
expand_url_via_redirect_header returned 'https://example.com/expanded-url' (the 302 Location header from the mock server)
Success marker /tmp/ssrf_success.txt created with content: SSRF_POC_SUCCESSFUL

reproduction.source_acquisition: run.sh clones the target repository on the host, checks out the pinned vulnerable commit, and mounts that checkout into the container at /target-repo.
